author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew X. 16.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Behold, I send you forth as sheep in the midst of wolves; be ye therefore wise as serpents, and harmless as doves.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Having made them feel confident about their necessary food, and opened unto them all men’s houses, and having invested their entrance with an appearance to attract veneration, charging them not to come in as wanderers, and beggars, but as much more venerable than those who received them (for this He signifies by His saying, “the workman is worthy of his hire;” and by His commanding them to inquire, who was worthy, and there to remain, and enjoining them to salute such as receive them; and by His threatening such as receive them not with those incurable evils): having I say, in this way cast out their anxiety, and armed them with the display of miracles, and made them as it were all iron and adamant, by delivering them from all worldly things, and enfranchising them from all temporal care: He speaks in what follows of the evils also that were to befall them; not only those that were to happen soon after, but those too that were to be in long course of time; from the first, even long beforehand, preparing them for the war against the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and many advantages were hence secured; and first, that they learnt the power of His foreknowledge; secondly, that no one should suspect, that through weakness of their Master came these evils upon them; thirdly, that such as undergo these things should not be dismayed by their falling out unexpectedly, and against hope; fourthly, that they might not at the very time of the cross be troubled on hearing these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed, they were just so affected at that time; when also He upbraided them, saying, “Because I have said these things unto you, sorrow hath filled your hearts; and none of you asketh me, whither goest Thou?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet He had said nothing as yet touching Himself, as that He should be bound, and scourged, and put to death, that He might not hereby also confound their minds; but for the present He announces before what should happen to themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, that they might learn that this system of war is new, and the manner of the array unwonted; as He sends them bare, and with one coat, and unshod, and without staff, and without girdle or scrip, and bids them be maintained by such as receive them; so neither here did He stay His speech, but to signify His unspeakable power, He saith, “Even thus setting out, exhibit the gentleness of “sheep,” and this, though ye are to go unto “wolves;” and not simply unto wolves, but “into the midst of wolves.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He bids them have not only gentleness as sheep, but also the harmlessness of the dove.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For thus shall I best show forth my might, when sheep get the better of wolves, and being in the midst of wolves, and receiving a thousand bites, so far from being consumed, do even work a change on them a thing far greater and more marvellous than killing them, to alter their spirit, and to reform their mind; and this, being only twelve, while the whole world is filled with the wolves.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then be ashamed, who do the contrary, who set like wolves upon our enemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so long as we are sheep, we conquer: though ten thousand wolves prowl around, we overcome and prevail.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if we become wolves, we are worsted, for the help of our Shepherd departs from us: for He feeds not wolves, but sheep: and He forsakes thee, and retires, for neither dost thou allow His might to be shown.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because, as He accounts the whole triumph His own, if thou being ill used, show forth gentleness; so if thou follow it up and give blows, thou obscurest His victory.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou consider, I pray thee, who they are that hear these injunctions, so hard and laborious: the timid and ignorant; the unlettered and uninstructed; such as are in every respect obscure, who have never been trained up in the Gentile laws, who do not readily present themselves in the public places; the fishermen, the publicans, men full of innumerable deficiencies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if these things were enough to confound even the lofty and great, how were they not enough to cast down and dismay them that were in all respects untried, and had never entertained any noble imagination?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But they did not cast them down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And very naturally,” some one may perhaps say; “because He gave them power to cleanse lepers, to drive out devils.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I would answer as follows: Nay, this very thing was enough especially to perplex them, that for all their raising the dead, they were to undergo these intolerable evils, both judgments, and executions, and the wars which all would wage on them, and the common hatred of the world; and that such terrors await them, while themselves are working miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is their consolation for all these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The power of Him that sends them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He puts this before all, saying, “Behold, I send you.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This suffices for your encouragement, this for confidence, and fearing none of your assailants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou authority? seest thou prerogative? seest thou invincible might?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now His meaning is like this: “Be not troubled” (so He speaks), “that sending you among wolves, I command you to be like sheep and like doves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For I might indeed have done the contrary, and have suffered you to undergo nothing terrible, nor as sheep to be exposed to wolves; I might have rendered you more formidable than lions; but it is expedient that so it should be.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This makes you also more glorious; this proclaims also my power.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This He said also unto Paul: “My grace is sufficient for thee, for my strength is made perfect in weakness.”“It is I, now mark it, who have caused you so to be.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in saying, “I send you forth as sheep,” He intimates this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Do not therefore despond, for I know, I know certainly, that in this way more than any other ye will be invincible to all.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, that they may contribute something on their own part also, and that all might not seem to be of His grace, nor they supposed to be crowned at random, and vainly, He saith, “Be ye therefore wise as serpents, and harmless as doves.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But what,” it might be said, “will our wisdom avail in so great dangers? nay, how shall we be able to have wisdom at all, when so many waves are drenching us all over?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For let a sheep be ever so wise, when it is in the midst of wolves, and so many wolves, what will it be able to do?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let the dove be ever so harmless, what will it profit, when so many hawks are assailing it?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the brutes indeed, not at all: but in you as much as possible.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But let us see what manner of wisdom He here requires.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That of the serpent, He saith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even as that animal gives up everything, and if its very body must be cut off, doth not very earnestly defend it, so that it may save its head; in like manner do thou also, saith He, give up every thing but the faith; though goods, body, life itself, must be yielded.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that is the head and the root; and if that be preserved, though thou lose all, thou wilt recover all with so much the more splendor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this account then He neither commanded to be merely a simple and single-hearted sort of person, nor merely wise; but hath mixed up both these, so that they may become virtue; taking in the wisdom of the serpent that we may not be wounded in our vitals; and the harmlessness of the dove, that we may not retaliate on our wrongdoers, nor avenge ourselves on them that lay snares; since wisdom again is useless, except this be added.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now what, I ask, could be more strict than these injunctions?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, was it not enough to suffer wrong?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, saith He, but I do not permit thee so much as to be indignant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is “the dove.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As though one should cast a reed into fire, and command it not to be burnt by the fire, but to quench it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: However, let us not be troubled; nay, for these things have come to pass, and have had an accomplishment, and have been shown in very deed, and men became wise as serpents, and harmless as doves; not being of another nature, but of the same with us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let not then any one account His injunctions impracticable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He beyond all others knows the nature of things; He knows that fierceness is not quenched by fierceness, but by gentleness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if in men’s actual deeds too thou wouldest see this result, read the book of the Acts of the Apostles, and thou wilt see how often, when the people of the Jews had risen up against them and were sharpening their teeth, these men, imitating the dove, and answering with suitable meekness, did away with their wrath, quenched their madness, broke their impetuosity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As when they said, “Did not we straitly command you, that ye should not speak in this name?”although able to work any number of miracles, they neither said nor did anything harsh, but answered for themselves with all meekness, saying, “Whether it be right to hearken unto you more than unto God, judge ye.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou seen the harmlessness of the dove?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Behold the wisdom of the serpent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For we cannot but speak the things, which we know and have heard.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how we must be perfect on all points, so as neither to be abased by dangers, nor provoked by anger?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore He said also,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Beware of men, for they shall deliver you up to councils, and they shall scourge you in their synagogues: and ye shall be brought before governors and kings for my sake, for a testimony to them and the Gentiles.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus again is He preparing them to be vigilant, in every case assigning to them the sufferance of wrong, and permitting the infliction of it to others; to teach thee that the victory is in suffering evil, and that His glorious trophies are thereby set up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He said not at all, “Fight ye also, and resist them that would vex you,” but only, “Ye shall suffer the utmost ills.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: O how great is the power of Him that speaks!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How great the self-command of them that hear!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed we have great cause to marvel, how they did not straightway dart away from Him on hearing these things, apt as they were to be startled at every sound, and such as had never gone further than that lake, around which they used to fish; and how they did not reflect, and say to themselves, “And whither after all this are we to flee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The courts of justice against us, the kings against us, the governors, the synagogues of the Jews, the nations of the Gentiles, the rulers, and the ruled.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: (For hereby He not only forewarned them of Palestine, and the ills therein, but discovered also the wars throughout the world, saying, “Ye shall be brought before kings and governors;” signifying that to the Gentiles also He was afterwards to send them as heralds.)

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Thou hast made the world our enemy, Thou hast armed against us all them that dwell on the earth, peoples, tyrants, kings.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what follows again is much more fearful, since men are to become on our account murderers of brothers, of children, of fathers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For the brother,” saith He, “shall deliver up the brother to death, and the father the child; and children shall rise up against their parents, and cause them to be put to death.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “How, then,” one might say, “will the rest of men believe, when they see on our account, children slain by their fathers, and brethren by brethren, and all things filled with abominations?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What? will not men, as though we were destructive demons, will they not, as though we were devoted, and pests of the world, drive us out from every quarter, seeing the earth filled with blood of kinsmen, and with so many murderers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Surely fair is the peace (is it not?) which we are to bring into men’s houses and give them, while we are filling those houses with so many slaughters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, had we been some great number of us, instead of twelve; had we been, instead of “unlearned and ignorant,” wise, and skilled in rhetoric, and mighty in speech; nay more, had we been even kings, and in possession of armies and abundance of wealth; how could we have persuaded any, while kindling up civil wars, yea, and other wars far worse than they?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, though we were to despise our own safety, which of all other men will give heed to us?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But none of these things did they either think or say, neither did they require any account of His injunctions, but simply yielded and obeyed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this came not from their own virtue only, but also of the wisdom of their Teacher.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For see how to each of the fearful things He annexed an encouragement; as in the case of such as received them not, He said, “It shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom and Gomorrha in the day of judgment, than for that city;” so here again, when He had said, “Ye shall be brought before governors and kings,” He added, “for my sake, for a testimony to them, and the Gentiles.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this is no small consolation, that they are suffering these things both for Christ, and for the Gentiles’ conviction.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus God, though no one regard, is found to be everywhere doing His own works.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now these things were a comfort to them, not that they desired the punishment of other men, but that they might have ground of confidence, as sure to have Him everywhere present with them, who had both foretold and foreknown these things; and because not as wicked men, and as pests, were they to suffer all this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And together with these, He adds another, and that no small consolation for them, saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But when they deliver you up, take no thoughthow or what ye shall speak, for it shall be given you in that hour what ye shall speak.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not ye that speak, but the Spirit of your Father that speaketh in you.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For lest they should say, “How shall we be able to persuade men, when such things are taking place?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He bids them be confident as to their defense also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And elsewhere indeed He saith, “I will give you a mouth and wisdom;”but here, “It is the Spirit of your Father that speaketh in you,” advancing them unto the dignity of the prophets.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, when He had spoken of the power that was given, then He added also the terrors, the murders, and the slaughters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For the brother shall deliver up the brother,” saith He, “to death, and the father the child, and the children shall rise up against their parents, and cause them to be put to death.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not even at this did He stop, but added also what was greatly more fearful, and enough to shiver a rock to pieces: “And ye shall be hated of all men.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And here again the consolation is at the doors, for, “For my name’s sake,” saith He, “ye shall suffer these things.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And with this again another, “But he that endureth to the end, the same shall be saved.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these things in another point of view likewise were sufficient to rouse up their spirits; since at any rate the power of their gospel was to blaze up so high, as that nature should be despised, and kindred rejected, and the Word preferred to all, chasing all mightily away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if no tyranny of nature is strong enough to withstand your sayings, but it is dissolved and trodden under foot, what else shall be able to get the better of you?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not, however, that your life will be in security, because these things shall be; but rather ye will have for your common enemies and foes them that dwell in the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Where now is Plato?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Where Pythagoras?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Where the long chainof the Stoics?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the first, after having enjoyed great honor, was so practically refuted, as even to be sold out of the country,and to succeed in none of his objects, no, not go much as in respect of one tyrant: yea, he betrayed his disciples, and ended his life miserably.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the Cynics, mere pollutions as they were, have all passed by like a dream and a shadow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet assuredly no such thing ever befell them, but rather they were accounted glorious for their heathen philosophy, and the Athenians made a public monument of the epistles of Plato, sent them by Dion; and they passed all their time at ease, and abounded in wealth not a little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, for instance, Aristippus was used to purchase costly harlots; and another made a will, leaving no common inheritance; and another, when his disciples had laid themselves down like a bridge, walked on them; and he of Sinope, they say, even behaved himself unseemly in the market place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, these are their honorable things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But there is no such thing here, but a strict temperance, and a perfect decency, and a war against the whole world in behalf of truth and godliness, and to be slain every day, and not until hereafter their glorious trophies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But there are some also, one may say, skilled in war amongst them; as Themistocles, Pericles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things too are children’s toys, compared with the acts of the fishermen.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what canst thou say?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That he persuaded the Athenians to embark in their ships, when Xerxes was marching upon Greece?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why in this case, when it is not Xerxes marching, but the devil with the whole world, and his evil spirits innumerable assailing these twelve men, not at one crisis only, but throughout their whole life, they prevailed and vanquished; and what was truly marvellous, not by slaying their adversaries, but by converting and reforming them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this especially you should observe throughout, that they slew not, nor destroyed such as were plotting against them, but having found them as bad as devils, they made them rivals of angels, enfranchising human nature from this evil tyranny, while as to those execrable demons that were confounding all things, they drave them out of the midst of markets, and houses, or rather even from the very wilderness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to this the choirs of the monks bear witness, whom they have planted everywhere, clearing out not the habitable only, but even the uninhabitable land.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is yet more marvellous, they did not this in fair conflict, but in the enduring of evil they accomplished it all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since men actually had them in the midst, twelve unlearned persons, binding, scourging, dragging them about, and were not able to stop their mouths; but as it is impossible to bind the sunbeam, so also their tongue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the reason was, “it was not they” themselves “that spake,” but the power of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus for instance did Paul overcome Agrippa, and Nero, who surpassed all men in wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For the Lord,” saith he, “stood with me, and strengthened me, and delivered me out of the mouth of the lion.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou also admire them, how when it was said to them, “Take no thought,” they yet believed, and accepted it, and none of the terrors amazed them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou say, He gave them encouragement enough, by saying, “It shall be the Spirit of your Father that shall speak;” even for this am I most amazed at them, that they doubted not, nor sought deliverance from their perils; and this, when not for two or three years were they to suffer these things, but all their life long.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the saying, “He that endureth to the end, the same shall be saved,” is an intimation of this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For His will is, that not His part only should be contributed, but that the good deeds should be also done of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Mark, for instance, how from the first, part is His, part His disciples’.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, to do miracles is His, but to provide nothing is theirs.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, to open all men’s houses, was of the grace from above; but to require no more than was needful, of their own self-denial.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For the workman is worthy of his hire.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Their bestowing peace was of the gift of God, their inquiring for the worthy, and not entering in without distinction unto all, of their own self command.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, to punish such as received them not was His, but to retire with gentleness from them, without reviling or insulting them, was of the apostles’ meekness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To give the Spirit, and cause them not to take thought, was of Him that sent them, but to become like sheep and doves, and to bear all things nobly, was of their calmness and prudence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To be hated and not to despond, and to endure, was their own; to save them that endured, was of Him who sent them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He said, “He that endureth to the end, the same shall be saved.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, because the more part are wont at the beginning indeed to be vehement, but afterwards to faint, therefore saith He, “I require the end.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what is the use of seeds, flourishing indeed at first, but a little after fading away?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore it is continued patience that He requires of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, lest any say, He wrought the whole Himself, and it was no wonder that they should prove such, suffering as they did nothing intolerable; therefore He saith unto them, “There is need also of patience on your part.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though I should rescue you from the first dangers, I am reserving you for others more grievous, and after these again others will succeed; and ye shall not cease to have snares laid for you, so long as ye have breath.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this He intimated in saying, “But he that endureth to the end, the same shall be saved.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause then, though He said, “Take no thought what ye shall speak;” yet elsewhere He saith, “Be ready to give an answer to every man that asketh you a reason of the hope that is in you.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, as long as the contest is among friends, He commands us also to take thought; but when there is a terrible tribunal, and frantic assemblies, and terrors on all sides, He bestows the influence from Himself, that they may take courage and speak out, and not be discouraged, nor betray the righteous cause.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in truth it was a very great thing, for a man occupied about lakes, and skins, and receipt of custom, when tyrants were on their thrones, and satraps, and guards standing by them, and the swords drawn, and all standing on their side; to enter in alone, bound, hanging down his head, and yet be able to open his mouth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed they allowed them neither speech nor defense with respect to their doctrines, but set about torturing them to death, as common pests of the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “They,” it is said, “that have turned the world upside down, are come hither also;” and again, “They preach things contrary to the decrees of Cæsar, saying that Jesus Christ is king.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And everywhere the courts of justice were preoccupied by such suspicions, and much influence from above was needed, for their showing both the truth of the doctrine they preached, and that they are not violating the common laws; so that they should neither, while earnest to speak of the doctrine, fall under suspicion of overturning the laws; nor again, while earnest to show that they were not overturning the common government, corrupt the perfection of their doctrines: all which thou wilt see accomplished with all due consideration, both in Peter and in Paul, and in all the rest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and as rebels and innovators, and revolutionists, they were accused all over the world; yet nevertheless they both repelled this impression, and invested themselves with the contrary, all men celebrating them as saviors, and guardians, and benefactors.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And all this they achieved by their much patience.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also Paul said, “I die daily;”and he continued to “stand in jeopardy” unto the end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then must we deserve, having such high patterns, and in peace giving way to effeminacy, and remissness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With none to make war (it is too evident) we are slain; we faint when no man pursues, in peace we are required to be saved, and even for this we are not sufficient.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they indeed, when the world was on fire, and the pile was being kindled over the whole earth, entering, snatched from within, out of the midst of the flame, such as were burning; but thou art not able so much as to preserve thyself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What confidence then will there be for us?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There are no stripes, no prisons, no rulers, no synagogues, nor aught else of that kind to set upon us; yea, quite on the contrary we rule and prevail.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both kings are godly, and there are many honors for Christians, and precedences, and distinctions, and immunities, and not even so do we prevail.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas they being daily led to execution, both teachers and disciples, and bearing innumerable stripes, and continual brandings, were in greater luxury than such as abide in Paradise; we who have endured no such thing, not even in a dream, are softer than any wax.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But they,” it will be said, “wrought miracles.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Did this then keep them from the scourge? did it free them from persecution?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, for this is the strange thing, that they suffered such things often even at the hands of them whom they benefited, and not even so were they confounded, receiving only evil for good.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But thou if thou bestow on any one any little benefit, and then be requited with anything unpleasant, art confounded, art troubled, and repentest of that which thou hast done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If now it should happen, as I pray it may not happen nor at any time fall out, that there be a war against churches, and a persecution, imagine how great will be the ridicule, how sore the reproaches.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And very naturally; for when no one exercises himself in the wrestling school, how shall he be distinguished in the contests?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What champion, not being used to the trainer, will be able, when summoned by the Olympic contests, to show forth anything great and noble against his antagonist?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Ought we not every day to wrestle and fight and run?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See ye not them that are called Pentathli, when they have no antagonists, how they fill a sack with much sand, and hanging it up try their full strength thereupon?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they that are still younger, practise the fight against their enemies upon the persons of their companions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These do thou also emulate, and practise the wrestlings of self denial.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed there are many that provoke to anger, and incite to lust, and kindle a great flame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Stand therefore against thy passions, bear nobly the mental pangs, that thou mayest endure also those of the body.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so the blessed Job, if he had not exercised himself well before his conflicts, would not have shone so brightly in the same.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Unless he had practised freedom from all despondency, he would have uttered some rash word, when his children died.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as it was he stood against all the assaults, against ruin of fortune, and destruction of so great affluence: against loss of children, against his wife’s commiseration, against plagues in body, against reproaches of friends, against revilings of servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wouldest see his ways of exercise also, hear him saying, how he used to despise wealth: “If I did but rejoice,” saith he, “because my wealth was great: if I set gold up for a heap, if I put my trust in a precious stone.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore neither was he confounded at their being taken away, since he desired them not when present.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear how he also managed what related to his children, not giving way to undue softness, as we do, but requiring of them all circumspection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he who offered sacrifice even for their secret sins, imagine how strict a judge he was of such as were manifest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wouldest also hear of his strivings after continence, hearken to him when he saith, “I made a covenant with mine eyes, that I should not think upon a maid.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause his wife did not break his spirit, for he loved her even before this, not however immoderately, but as is due to a wife.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I am led even to marvel, whence it came into the devil’s thought to stir up the contest, knowing as he did of his previous training.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence then did it occur to him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The monster is wicked, and never despairs: and this turns out to us a very great condemnation that he indeed never gives up the hope of our destruction, but we despair of our own salvation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But for bodily mutilation and indignity, mark how he practised himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, inasmuch as he himself had never undergone any such thing, but had continued to live in wealth and luxury, and in all other splendor, he used to divine other men’s calamities, one by one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this he declared, when he said, “For the thing which I greatly feared is come upon me; and that which I was afraid of is come unto me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, “But I wept for every helpless man, and groaned when I saw a man in distress.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So because of this, nothing of what happened confounded him, none of those great and intolerable ills.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For I bid thee not look at the ruin of his substance, nor at the loss of his children, nor at that incurable plague, nor at his wife’s device against him; but at those things which are far more grievous than these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And what,” saith one, “did Job suffer more grievous than these? for from his history there is nothing more than these for us to learn.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because we are asleep, we do not learn, since he surely that is anxious, and searches well for the pearl, will know of many more particulars than these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the more grievous, and apt to infuse greater perplexity, were different.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And first, his knowing nothing certain about the kingdom of heaven, and the resurrection; which indeed he also spoke of, lamenting.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For I shall not live alway, that I should suffer long.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Next, his being conscious to himself of many good works.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thirdly, his being conscious of no evil thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Fourthly, his supposing that at God’s hands he was undergoing it; or if at the devil’s, this again was enough to offend him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Fifthly, his hearing his friends accusing him of wickedness, “For thou hast not been scourged,” say they, “according to what thy sins deserve.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Sixthly, his seeing such as lived in wickedness prospering, and exulting over him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seventhly, not having any other to whom he might look as even having ever suffered such things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wouldest learn how great these things are, consider our present state.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if now, when we are looking for a kingdom, and hoping for a resurrection, and for the unutterable blessings, and are conscious to ourselves of countless evil deeds, and when we have so many examples, and are partakers of so high a philosophy; should any persons lose a little gold, and this often, after having taken it by violence, they deem life not to be lived in, having no wife to lay sore on them, nor bereaved of children, nor reproached by friends, nor insulted by servants, but rather having many to comfort them, some by words, some by deeds; of how noble crowns must not he be worthy, who seeing what he had gotten together by honest labor, snatched away from him for nought and at random, and after all that, undergoing temptations without number, like sleet, yet throughout all abides unmoved, and offers to the Lord his due thanksgiving for it all?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, though no one had spoken any of the other taunts, yet his wife’s words alone were sufficient utterly to shake a very rock.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Look, for example, at her craft.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: No mention of money, none of camels, and flocks, and herds, (for she was conscious of her husband’s self command with regard to these), but of what was harder to bear than all these, I mean, their children; and she deepens the tragedy, and adds to it her own influence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if when men were in wealth, and suffering no distress, in many things and oft have women prevailed on them: imagine how courageous was that soul, which repulsed her, assaulting him with such powerful weapons, and which trod under foot the two most tyrannical passions, desire and pity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet many having conquered desire, have yielded to pity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That noble Joseph, for instance, held in subjection the most tyrannical of pleasures, and repulsed that strange woman, plying him as she did with innumerable devices; but his tears he contained not, but when he saw his brethren that had wronged him, he was all on fire with that passion, and quickly cast off the mask, and discovered the part he had been playing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when first of all she is his wife, and when her words are piteous, and the moment favorable for her, as well as his wounds and his stripes, and those countless waves of calamities; how can one otherwise than rightly pronounce the soul impassive to so great a storm to be firmer than any adamant?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Allow me freely to say, that the very apostles, if not inferior to this blessed man, are at least not greater than he was.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they indeed were comforted by the suffering for Christ; and this medicine was so sufficient daily to relieve them, that the Lord puts it everywhere, saying, “for me, for my sake,” and, “If they call me, the master of the house, Beelzebub.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But he was destitute of this encouragement, and of that from miracles, and of that from grace; for neither had he so great power of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is yet greater, nourished in much delicacy, not from amongst fishermen, and publicans, and such as lived frugally, but after enjoyment of so much honor, he suffered all that he did suffer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what seemed hardest to bear in the case of the apostles, this same he also underwent, being hated of friends, of servants, of enemies, of them who had received kindness of him: and the sacred anchor, the harbor without waves, namely, that which was said to the apostles, “for my sake,” of this he had no sight.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I admire again the three children, for that they dared the furnace, that they stood up against a tyrant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But hear what they say, “We serve not thy Gods, nor worship the image which thou hast set up.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A thing which was the greatest encouragement to them, to know of a certainty that for God they are suffering all whatsoever they suffer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this man knew not that it was all conflicts, and a wrestling; for had he known it, he would not have felt what was happening.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At any rate, when he heard, “Thinkest thou that I have uttered to thee mine oracles for nought, or that thou mightest be proved righteous?”consider how straightway, at a bare word, he breathed again, how he made himself of no account, how he accounted himself not so much as to have suffered what he had suffered, thus saying, “Why do I plead any more, being admonished and reproved of the Lord, hearing such things, I being nothing?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, “I have heard of Thee before, as far as hearing of the ear; but now mine eye hath seen Thee; wherefore I have made myself vile, and have melted away; and I accounted myself earth and ashes.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This fortitude then, this moderation, of him that was before law and grace, let us also emulate, who are after law and grace; that we may also be able to share with him the eternal tabernacles; unto which may we all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be the glory and the victory forever and ever.
